text.fullText: From: deb.shankland@everyactioncustom.com To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 1/29/2026 1:33:23 PM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 -------------------------------------------- [You don't often get email from deb.shankland@everyactioncustom.com. Learn why this is important at https://aka.ms/LearnAboutSenderIdentification ] Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Dear Administrator Lee Zeldin, I live in the Great Lakes watershed, specifically Lake Erie. I know well the history of a "nearly dead" Lake Erie, and the once terrible state of the Cuyahoga River, an Erie tributary. I witnessed and smelled the dead fish, the greasy sheen, and felt the shame of being part of "the Mistake on the Lake". Fortunately, I also witnessed and participated in the long recovery (over 50 years) of these waterbodies, thanks to the Clean Water Act (CWA). Now, both water bodies are the "places to be", fomenting thriving businesses along their banks, and adding to (instead of subtracting from) our health and quality of life. This is not the time to go back. In the last 50 years, there has been tremendous growth and development in my watershed and others in the Great Lakes region. This is not entirely positive: it has decreased green space and increased impervious surfaces. This makes protection of the little remaining green space, including wetlands and small tributary streams, imperative. Flooding is increasing due to global warming as well as the marked increase of impervious surfaces. The U.S. EPA should not only continue to enforce environmental laws and defend the CWA, but should actually INCREASE protections in order to protect our health and the economy by reducing flooding and pollution. The proposed rule change to Section 401 will instead weaken protections by removing control from states and Tribes. This is foolish and incredibly short-sighted. Further, this proposed rule change will strip protections of wetlands, which, for free, support recreational activities that bring in $50 billion annually to our region and filter pollutants, in addition to controlling flooding. Everyone, no matter their background or party affiliation, needs clean water and a functioning environment to survive. Everyone supports protections of our water resources, from intermittent streams to wetlands to our entire Great Lakes. Do not go backwards--it was awful. In support of our state and Tribal rights, I strongly urge you to fully enforce the CWA, strengthen it, and maintain the authority of states and Tribes to protect clean water. Sincerely, Debra Brecksville, OH
